statement.markdown: You are given a grid with dimensions n rows and m columns. You can move either right or down from any cell. The goal is to find the number of paths from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell. Write a function `solve` that counts these paths. The function should return the number of paths as an integer.
statement.inputFormat: Input will be an integer n representing the number of rows in the grid, followed by the number of columns `m` in the grid.
statement.outputFormat: Return the number of paths as an integer.
statement.notes: The grid is initially empty. Each movement (either right or down) increases the path length.

constraints.description: Number of rows in the grid
Number of columns in the grid

examples.explanation: One possible path is right, right, down, down, right. There are 6 such paths.
There are 30 paths in this grid.

hints.text: Notice that this problem can be solved using dynamic programming.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by counting the number of unique combinations of right and down movements that sum up to the total number of movements.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force solution would be to use DFS or BFS with a tree/graph data structure and count the number of paths.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use dynamic programming to store and reuse the number of paths to each cell.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the number of paths to the cell at position (i, j).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that the dp[i][j] stores the correct number of paths to the cell at position (i, j).
editorial.dryRun: To verify the optimal solution, you can use a 2D array and fill in the dp[i][j] values manually.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to handle the base cases.
Incorrectly calculating the number of paths.
